visual_checks: Look for ARIA landmark roles (banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo, search, form, region) identifying page regions
Check if UI components use appropriate autocomplete attributes on input fields for common personal data (name, email, address, phone)
Verify that icons and visual indicators have programmatically determinable purpose through ARIA labels or semantic HTML
Look for links and buttons whose purpose can be determined from their accessible name or surrounding context
Check if page regions such as header, nav, main, aside, and footer use proper HTML5 semantic elements or ARIA roles
Look for input fields collecting user information and verify they use the correct autocomplete token values
pass_conditions: Page regions are identified using HTML5 landmark elements or ARIA landmark roles
Input fields for common personal information use appropriate autocomplete attribute values (e.g., autocomplete='name', 'email', 'tel', 'street-address')
The purpose of icons, links, and buttons can be programmatically determined through accessible names or semantic markup
All major page sections (header, navigation, main content, footer) have identifiable purposes via landmarks
UI components that collect standardized user data use recognized autocomplete tokens per HTML specification
fail_conditions: Page regions lack ARIA landmarks or HTML5 semantic elements, making their purpose indeterminate
Input fields collecting personal data (name, email, phone, address) are missing autocomplete attributes
Icons convey meaning but have no programmatically determinable purpose (no aria-label, no text alternative)
Navigation, header, and footer regions are built with generic div elements and no landmark roles
Autocomplete attribute values are incorrect or do not match the type of data the field collects
na_conditions: Page contains no identifiable regions, input fields, or UI components whose purpose needs to be programmatically determined
Page is a minimal document with a single content area and no form inputs
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Page failed to load so landmarks and autocomplete attributes cannot be evaluated
common_mistakes: Using generic div or span containers for major page regions instead of semantic HTML5 elements or ARIA landmarks
Omitting autocomplete attributes on form fields that collect standard personal information
Using incorrect autocomplete token values that do not match the field purpose (e.g., autocomplete='name' on an email field)
Having multiple banner or main landmarks on a single page without distinguishing labels
Icons or image buttons with purely visual meaning but no programmatic label
Using role='region' without an accessible name, making the landmark meaningless

examples.pass: A page uses <header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>, and <footer> elements for page structure, and all personal data input fields include correct autocomplete attributes such as autocomplete='given-name' and autocomplete='email'
examples.fail: A page uses <div class='header'> and <div class='nav'> with no ARIA landmark roles, and form fields collecting name and email have no autocomplete attributes
examples.partial: Landmark roles are correctly applied to major page regions, but several input fields for address and phone number are missing autocomplete attributes

plain_meaning: At Level AAA, the purpose of UI components, icons, and page regions must be programmatically determinable -- typically through ARIA roles, landmarks, or semantic tokens -- so that personalization tools can adapt the presentation (e.g., replace icons with familiar symbols, highlight a specific region type). The intent is to support users with cognitive disabilities who use tools that re-skin or simplify pages based on identified purpose.
scope_applies_to: UI components whose purpose could be signaled via ARIA roles or microdata (buttons, links, navigation items)
Page regions that map to landmark roles (banner, navigation, main, contentinfo, complementary, search)
Icons whose meaning could be identified via semantic markup
Any page evaluated at Level AAA scope (the criterion does not depend on the site claiming a conformance level)
scope_does_not_apply_to: Reviews scoped to Level A or AA only (1.3.6 is a Level AAA criterion)
Structural relationships within content -- that belongs to SC 1.3.1
Text alternatives for non-text content -- that belongs to SC 1.1.1
Input field purpose -- that belongs to SC 1.3.5
borderline_calibration: SC 1.3.6 is a Level AAA criterion: evaluate it whenever the review scope includes AAA. It does NOT depend on the site claiming any conformance level — never condition the verdict or rationale on a claimed or assumed conformance level.
Presence of landmark roles / HTML5 semantic regions on header/nav/main/footer is a strong signal the criterion is met.
Missing landmark roles on major page regions is a failure at medium+.
The criterion is intentionally broad and under-specified; prefer info/note severity over hard fails unless there is clear neglect of programmatic purpose.
auditor_anti_patterns: Do NOT assume, infer, or assert that the site claims any WCAG conformance level. Only an explicit, published accessibility-conformance statement counts as a conformance claim. The string 'AAA' or 'AA' appearing in CSS, class names, color hex values, or code is NOT a conformance claim. Never base a finding or its rationale on an assumed conformance claim.
Do NOT flag overly long, verbose, or redundant accessible names under 1.3.6 — accessible-name quality belongs to SC 2.4.4 / 4.1.2, not Identify Purpose.

role='presentation' on a nested structural wrapper inside a CMS template is not automatically a 1.3.6 failure — judge whether the page's REGIONS have a determinable purpose via the top-level landmarks, not every nested wrapper.
